articles.title: No profit, no project: Reynoso tries to demystify development
articles.narrative: This article discusses Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso's conditional recommendation for the Monitor Point development, a proposed 1,150-unit project on the Greenpoint waterfront. Reynoso's recommendation uniquely includes specific financial details, such as the 6.25% to 6.5% yield required by financiers for initial capital lending, to illustrate the economic realities of housing development. He emphasizes that developers need to generate profit to secure funding, and that public land projects like Monitor Point, which require upfront and ongoing costs to the developer, differ from those where land is transferred at nominal cost. Despite this detailed financial explanation, Reynoso ultimately aligns with the local Council member's demand for 50% affordable units, suggesting a mix of moderate-income households to make the project financially viable and socially diverse.

data_points.notable_features: Proposed 1,150-unit project on the Greenpoint waterfront, including market-rate and affordable units, a Greenpoint Monitor Museum, public realm amenities, park maintenance, and a new MTA bus-washing facility.
data_points.project_notes: Initial lending contingent on developers demonstrating a 6.25% to 6.5% yield on initial investment. The MTA site requires upfront and ongoing costs from the developer, which need to be offset by market-rate units. The project is currently undergoing a rezoning process, with the City Council scheduled to vote next month. If rezoning is rejected, a smaller as-of-right project would still require the developer to build an MTA bus-washing facility and pay $29 million in ground rent.

articles.title: “It’s going to hurt”: NYC resi players push back on proposed cash sales tax
articles.narrative: New York state lawmakers are considering a new 1% tax on residential cash sales of $1 million or more in New York City, a proposal projected to generate $160 million annually to help fund the city's budget deficit. This potential tax, along with a recently announced annual levy on second homes valued at $5 million or more, has drawn significant criticism from real estate professionals. Industry experts express concern that the lack of clear details and the cumulative effect of these taxes could deter transactions, particularly given that cash deals accounted for 65% of all sales in the city last year, rising to 75% for sales over $1 million. Critics also suggest the projected revenue may be overstated, as it doesn't account for potential changes in buyer behavior or market activity.

articles.title: NY Dirt: Latest pied-à-terre update like “a night at improv”
articles.narrative: New York Governor Kathy Hochul has proposed a two-step plan to implement a pied-à-terre tax on second homes, primarily targeting condos and co-ops with a "market value" of at least $1 million, which the Department of Finance estimates to be equivalent to a $5 million sales value. The second step, to be enacted two years later, involves creating an entirely new metric for valuing co-op and condo sales. Critics argue that the proposed valuation methods are unclear and inconsistent with actual sales prices, highlighting the complexity of implementing such a tax. The plan is seen by some as a deferral of a clear policy, raising doubts about the city's ability to develop a robust valuation system within the two-year timeframe.

articles.title: NYC’s top deals: Brooklyn Heights townhouse sells for $13M
articles.narrative: Extell Development Company has acquired a significant development site at 405-407 Park Avenue in Midtown for approximately $451 million. The transaction involved multiple sellers, including Corem Property Group AB and a joint venture between Deutsche Bank's asset management arm and MRP Realty. This acquisition also includes air rights from the nearby Central Synagogue, indicating a strategic move for future development in a prime Manhattan location.

data_points.address: 405-407 Park Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.project_notes: Sellers were reportedly seeking north of $500 million for the site. Extell also acquired air rights from the nearby Central Synagogue.

articles.title: Can JLL smooth out Sundance Square leasing as Sasha and Ed Bass’ step back?
articles.narrative: JLL has been appointed to manage retail leasing for Sundance Square, a 35-block mixed-use district in Downtown Fort Worth. This move follows criticism of the previous stewardship by Sasha and Ed Bass, who took control in 2019 and faced challenges including high vacancy rates and tenant complaints about untenable rent increases and unfulfilled marketing services. The Basses also appointed LanCarte Commercial Real Estate to handle office leasing a few months prior, signaling a shift in management strategy for the prominent downtown area.

data_points.notable_features: 35-block mixed-use district in Downtown Fort Worth.
data_points.project_notes: Previous stewardship by Sasha and Ed Bass since 2019 faced criticism for high vacancy rates and tenant issues.
